sections.heading: Program Overview
sections.body: The Bridges Program serves youth aged 14 to 24 transitioning out of high school with co-occurring mental health and life skills needs. Q1 2026 enrolled 47 active participants across the King Edward Park and Mill Woods sites, with 12 youth on the waitlist as of March 31.

sections.heading: Outcomes
sections.body:
Of the 47 active participants, 38 completed the quarterly check-in survey. Reported outcomes include 84% improved daily functioning since intake, 76% reduced anxiety symptoms, 71% would recommend the program to a friend, and average self-reported wellbeing scores increased from 4.2 to 6.8 out of 10.

Participant feedback was overwhelmingly positive. Several youth described the program as the only thing keeping them showing up. Another wrote that Bridges was a place where she actually felt heard for the first time since her mother passed.

sections.heading: Staffing
sections.body: The program is delivered by 3 full-time youth workers and 1 part-time clinical supervisor. We onboarded one new youth worker in February following the unexpected departure of a staff member in January. Staff retention remains a concern given Edmonton's competitive nonprofit labour market and our compensation falling below sector benchmarks.

sections.heading: Concerns
sections.body: The waitlist has grown 22% since Q4 2025. Without additional capacity, average wait time will exceed 8 weeks by Q3 2026. We are also seeing increased acuity at intake, with more participants reporting active suicidal ideation than in any previous quarter on record.

sections.heading: Recommendations
sections.body: We recommend exploring a third site location in north Edmonton, increasing clinical supervision hours from 0.5 FTE to 1.0 FTE, and revisiting compensation benchmarks for frontline staff.

sections.heading: Program Overview
sections.body: The Anchor Program provides drop-in mental health peer support for youth aged 16 to 22. Q1 2026 logged 312 unique visits across 14 weeks of programming at our Whyte Avenue location.

sections.heading: Outcomes
sections.body:
Outcome data has been difficult to collect this quarter. Our intake survey response rate dropped to 34%, down sharply from 61% in Q4 2025. Of those who completed the survey, 58% reported the program was somewhat helpful, 22% reported it was very helpful, 14% reported it was not helpful, and 6% declined to answer.

Several youth raised concerns about wait times to speak with a peer support worker, with three separate complaints documented in the suggestion box. One youth wrote that the staff seem burnt out and rushed and another said it felt like nobody had time anymore.

sections.heading: Staffing
sections.body: Anchor is delivered by 2 full-time peer support workers and a rotation of 4 trained volunteers. We have struggled to fill an open peer support position since November 2025, now five months vacant. The team is operating at roughly 70% capacity with no end in sight.

sections.heading: Concerns
sections.body:
Staff burnout is the most pressing issue I can identify. Both full-time workers have indicated they are considering leaving if conditions don't improve. I have flagged this to the Executive Director on three separate occasions since January and have not received a clear response.

The program is also facing a proposed 18% funding reduction in the 2026-27 budget, which I want to formally raise concern about. We discussed this in the March leadership meeting but I do not believe the implications for service delivery were fully understood at that time.

sections.heading: Recommendations
sections.body: Immediate hiring of a third peer support worker. Reconsideration of the proposed funding reduction. Wellness support and retention bonuses for current staff before we lose them.

sections.heading: Program Overview
sections.body: Compass delivers school-based mental health workshops in partnership with Edmonton Public Schools and Edmonton Catholic Schools. Q1 2026 reached 1,847 students across 12 schools through 84 workshop sessions.

sections.heading: Outcomes
sections.body:
Pre and post workshop surveys, administered to 1,612 students, showed 91% reported learning at least one new coping skill, 87% rated the workshop as good or excellent, and 79% indicated they would feel comfortable seeking help if needed. A small but important 4% reported feeling worse after the workshop, which prompted same-day follow-up referrals to school counsellors per our protocol.

Teacher feedback from 58 respondents was strongly positive, with 95% requesting return visits next school year. One teacher wrote that the workshops were the most engaged she had seen her students all year.

sections.heading: Staffing
sections.body: Compass is delivered by 5 part-time workshop facilitators with combined 2.4 FTE plus the program manager. Staffing has been stable throughout the quarter. Our facilitators are predominantly graduate students from the University of Alberta and MacEwan University, which provides good talent flow but creates expected turnover at academic year transitions.

sections.heading: Concerns
sections.body: None significant this quarter. The program is performing well within budget and on schedule. We continue to monitor the small percentage of students who report negative experiences and have refined our screening protocol accordingly.

sections.heading: Recommendations
sections.body: Expand to an additional 4 schools in the 2026-27 academic year. Consider piloting a parent-focused workshop series based on repeated requests from school administrators and counsellors.

sections.heading: Total Operating Revenue 2025
sections.body: Northbridge's 2025 operating revenue of $2,847,000 came from a diversified funder base. Government of Alberta Community and Social Services contributed $1,120,000 (39%). United Way of the Alberta Capital Region contributed $485,000 (17%). Edmonton Community Foundation contributed $312,000 (11%). Private foundation grants totaled $428,000 across six grants. Individual donations totaled $287,000 from approximately 1,400 donors. Corporate sponsorship and event revenue contributed $215,000.

sections.heading: Program Allocations 2025
sections.body: Bridges Program: $987,000 (35% of program spending). Anchor Program: $642,000 (23%). Compass Program: $891,000 (31%). Administration and shared costs: $327,000 (11%).

sections.heading: 2026-27 Proposed Budget Changes
sections.body: The proposed budget reflects a strategic reallocation discussed at the March 12 leadership meeting. Bridges receives a 12% increase to $1,105,000 to fund waitlist reduction. Compass receives an 8% increase to $962,000 to support school expansion. Anchor receives an 18% reduction to $527,000 due to reduced demonstrable outcomes per dollar based on Q4 2025 metrics.

sections.heading: Financial Observations
sections.body: I want to flag for the board that 2025 saw three reimbursement irregularities in the Bridges program that have now been resolved but warrant continued monitoring. Petty cash reconciliation in November and December showed gaps totaling $1,847 which were attributed to administrative error and replaced from operational funds. I recommend implementing dual-approval for all petty cash above $200 going forward.

sections.heading: Audit Status
sections.body: Our annual audit by Hampton Pinder LLP is scheduled for completion by May 30, 2026. Preliminary review has not flagged any material concerns.

sections.heading: Active Staff Headcount: 19
sections.body:
Bridges Program (4): Tara Whitford, Coordinator, FT, hired March 2023. Marcus Lee, Youth Worker, FT, hired June 2024. Aisha Rahman, Youth Worker, FT, hired February 2026. Jordan Petit, Clinical Supervisor, 0.5 FTE, hired August 2024.

Anchor Program (2 of 3 budgeted): Devon Ng, Program Lead, FT, hired November 2022. Sam Cheung, Peer Support Worker, FT, hired April 2024. VACANT — Peer Support Worker position open since November 2025.

Compass Program (6): Priya Sharma, Manager, FT, hired January 2023. Rachel Owusu, Facilitator, 0.6 FTE. Kim Tran, Facilitator, 0.5 FTE. Daniel Webb, Facilitator, 0.5 FTE. Mei Zhang, Facilitator, 0.4 FTE. Liam O'Connor, Facilitator, 0.4 FTE.

Administration and Leadership (5): Heather Reimer, Executive Director, FT, hired August 2019. Marcus Kowalski, Finance Director, FT, hired June 2021. Janelle Brooks, HR Coordinator, FT. Vacant — Communications Coordinator, since January 2026. Brent Halloway, Operations Manager, FT, hired October 2020.

sections.heading: Departures Q1 2026
sections.body: Kira Henderson, Bridges Youth Worker, departed January 17, 2026 (cited workload and pay). Asha Drummond, Communications Coordinator, departed January 31, 2026 (relocated).

sections.heading: Notes
sections.body: Two open positions have been unfilled for over three months. Staff turnover this quarter sits at 10.5%, slightly above sector average of 8%. Exit interview themes include compensation below market, workload intensity, and limited career progression pathways.

sections.heading: Confidential — Internal HR Use
sections.body: This log records formal disciplinary actions taken in Q1 2026. Verbal coaching conversations are not included.

sections.heading: Actions
sections.body:
January 14, 2026 — Bridges Program staff member received a written warning for repeated late arrivals affecting program start times. Performance improvement plan initiated. Status: Improved, plan closed March 30.

February 8, 2026 — Anchor Program staff member received a verbal warning, documented per policy, regarding inappropriate communication with a youth participant on personal social media. Status: Resolved, no recurrence.

February 22, 2026 — Bridges Program staff member received a written warning for failing to document a Section 4 incident report within the required 24-hour window. Refresher training completed. Status: Resolved.

March 4, 2026 — Operations staff member was placed on suspension with pay pending investigation regarding expense reimbursement irregularities. Investigation concluded March 18 with finding of administrative error rather than misconduct. Staff member returned to work with revised reimbursement procedures in place.

March 19, 2026 — Anchor Program staff member received a verbal warning regarding peer conflict with another team member. Mediation session held. Status: Ongoing monitoring.

sections.heading: Pattern Notes
sections.body: Three of five Q1 disciplinary actions involved frontline program staff. Two involved Anchor Program team members specifically. HR recommends a team-level review of Anchor Program working conditions in light of the staffing report and burnout concerns flagged separately.

org.title: Bridges Case Summary — Anonymized Cohort Analysis Q1 2026
org.author: Jordan Petit, Clinical Supervisor

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.body: This document summarizes anonymized clinical themes across 47 active Bridges participants in Q1 2026. All identifying information has been removed. This document does not constitute a clinical record and is for program planning purposes only.

sections.heading: Presenting Concerns at Intake
sections.body: Of the 47 participants, 32 presented with primary concerns related to anxiety, often co-occurring with school avoidance or social withdrawal. 28 presented with depressive symptoms ranging from mild to severe. 14 reported a history of self-harm. 11 disclosed active suicidal ideation at intake, which is the highest count we have recorded in any quarter. 9 reported substance use concerns. 6 had recently exited foster care. 4 disclosed experiences of family violence.

sections.heading: Engagement Patterns
sections.body: Average session attendance was 78%, comparable to historical norms. Participants who engaged in the optional peer mentorship component had 12% higher engagement than those who did not. Session-level dropout occurred most frequently in the first three weeks, consistent with prior cohorts.

sections.heading: Outcomes at 12 Weeks
sections.body: Of the 47 participants, 38 completed standardized outcome measures. Mean GAD-7 scores decreased from 14.2 at intake to 9.1 at 12 weeks. Mean PHQ-9 scores decreased from 13.8 at intake to 8.4 at 12 weeks. These changes are clinically meaningful.

sections.heading: Clinical Observations
sections.body: The cohort shows higher acuity at intake than previous quarters. Suicidal ideation disclosures have nearly doubled compared to Q1 2025 when 6 participants disclosed at intake. This may reflect either a true increase in community need or increased comfort disclosing in our intake process. Either interpretation warrants increased clinical capacity.

sections.heading: Recommendations
sections.body: Increase clinical supervision allocation. Consider piloting a brief crisis intervention protocol within Bridges for participants disclosing active ideation rather than referring out, given Edmonton's strained crisis services landscape.

org.title: Internal Email — Heather to Marcus on Anchor Program Funding
org.author: Heather Reimer, Executive Director

sections.heading: From: Heather Reimer to Marcus Kowalski — March 6, 2026
sections.body:
Subject: Anchor reallocation — board prep

Marcus,

Following up on our conversation Tuesday. I want to make sure the board materials clearly frame the Anchor reduction as a strategic reallocation rather than a programmatic failure. Devon will push back hard on this and I want our numbers airtight.

Can you put together a one-pager showing cost-per-outcome across all three programs? Compass and Bridges are going to look very strong on that metric. Anchor is harder to quantify because peer support outcomes don't fit neatly into our framework, but that's exactly the point we need to make to the board.

Also, between us, the Anchor situation is more complicated than the budget alone. Devon has been very vocal about staffing and I want to be careful that we're not seen as cutting a program because its lead has been raising concerns. We need to lead with the data.

One more thing. Brent mentioned the November petty cash situation again. I thought we had closed that but he's still uneasy. Can you walk him through the documentation one more time before the board meeting? I don't want any surprises.

Thanks,
Heather

org.title: Internal Email — Brent to Heather on Reimbursement Concerns
org.author: Brent Halloway, Operations Manager

sections.heading: From: Brent Halloway to Heather Reimer — March 11, 2026
sections.body:
Subject: Following up — petty cash and reimbursements

Heather,

I want to put my concerns in writing so we have a record.

The petty cash discrepancies in November and December 2025 totalled $1,847 across the Bridges program. They were attributed to administrative error and the funds were replaced from operational reserves. Marcus has shown me documentation that satisfies the audit standard, but I remain concerned about the pattern.

Three things specifically. First, the same staff member was responsible for the petty cash reconciliation in both months where discrepancies appeared. Second, two of the receipts that were eventually located showed signatures that don't match handwriting samples we have on file from that staff member. Third, the staff member in question was the one placed on suspension in March pending investigation, which I understand was closed as administrative error.

I am not making any accusation here. I am noting that we have a pattern, that the investigation outcome surprised me, and that I believe we should implement the dual-approval recommendation Marcus mentioned in the funding summary as soon as possible rather than waiting for budget approval.

I would also like to discuss whether an external review by our auditors is warranted before the May audit.

Brent

sections.heading: Background
sections.body: In partnership with the Bissell Centre, Northbridge piloted a referral pathway for youth aging out of Bissell's family services into our Bridges and Anchor programs. The pilot ran from June 2025 through January 2026 and served 18 youth.

sections.heading: Outcomes
sections.body:
Of the 18 referred youth, 16 completed an intake, 14 began programming, and 11 were retained at 12 weeks. Retention rate of 79% is strong for this population.

Of the 11 retained participants, outcome measures showed 9 with clinically meaningful improvement on standardized measures, 1 with stable scores, and 1 with worsening scores who was referred for higher-acuity care.

sections.heading: Qualitative Themes
sections.body: Participants from this cohort consistently named housing instability as their primary stressor, often above mental health concerns. Several participants noted that the combination of Bissell's housing supports with Northbridge's mental health programming felt more coherent than navigating separate systems. One youth wrote that for the first time it felt like the people helping her actually talked to each other.

sections.heading: Recommendations
sections.body: Formalize the referral partnership with a memorandum of understanding. Explore co-located service delivery at the Bissell Whyte Avenue location. This pilot has the strongest outcome data of any partnership initiative we have undertaken in the past three years.

sections.heading: Section 4 Reportable Incidents
sections.body:
The following incidents meet the threshold for Section 4 reporting under Northbridge's Safety and Disclosure Policy. All have been reported to the relevant authorities where required.

Incident 26-001 — January 9, 2026 (Bridges): A 16-year-old participant disclosed active suicidal ideation with plan during a one-on-one session. Crisis protocol activated. Participant accompanied to emergency department by staff. Returned to programming February 14 with safety plan in place. Case closed.

Incident 26-002 — January 22, 2026 (Anchor): Verbal altercation between two participants in drop-in space. No physical contact. Resolved without further incident.

Incident 26-003 — February 11, 2026 (Bridges): Staff member observed bruising on a 14-year-old participant. Mandatory report filed with Children's Services. Investigation ongoing. Participant continues in program with additional support.

Incident 26-004 — March 2, 2026 (Anchor): Participant disclosed recent self-harm. Safety plan developed in session. Referred to family physician and Bridges program for higher-intensity support.

Incident 26-005 — March 18, 2026 (Bridges): A participant did not arrive for scheduled session and could not be reached for 48 hours. Wellness check requested through EPS non-emergency. Participant located safely. Returned to programming March 22.

sections.heading: Trends
sections.body: Q1 2026 shows 5 reportable incidents, compared to 3 in Q4 2025 and 4 in Q1 2025. The increase is consistent with the higher acuity at intake reported separately by Bridges. No incidents to date have involved staff conduct concerns.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.body: Pre and post workshop surveys were administered to 1,612 of 1,847 students reached in Q1 2026. Teacher surveys were administered to all 58 partnering classroom teachers.

sections.heading: Student Themes — Positive
sections.body:
Students consistently appreciated the use of relatable examples, the option to participate anonymously through written reflection, and the inclusion of practical coping techniques they could use the same day. Many noted that mental health was rarely discussed openly in their school environment and that the workshop normalized seeking help.

Selected anonymous student feedback: "the workshop made me feel like I'm not the only one," "I learned that anxiety isn't weakness," and "the breathing exercise actually helped during my math test."

sections.heading: Student Themes — Critical
sections.body:
A small percentage of students reported feeling worse after the workshop. Common themes included feeling exposed by content that hit too close to home, frustration that the workshop ended without follow-up support, and one student wrote "talking about feelings just made me feel them more without anywhere to put them."

These responses prompted same-day referrals to school counselling and a refinement of our exit protocol.

sections.heading: Teacher Themes
sections.body: Teachers were uniformly positive. 95% requested return visits. One teacher noted that several students approached her privately in the days after the workshop to disclose concerns they had not previously raised.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.body: This brief informs major donors and grantors about Q1 2026 highlights and emerging priorities. Distribution: top 40 donors and active grantors.

sections.heading: Key Messages
sections.body:
Northbridge served over 2,200 youth across our three programs in Q1 2026. We expanded our school-based reach to 12 schools through the Compass program, reaching 1,847 students with mental health workshops. Our intensive Bridges program achieved a 22% reduction in average anxiety scores among participating youth.

We are facing two challenges that we want to be transparent about with our donor community. First, we are seeing higher acuity at intake than in any previous quarter, particularly in our Bridges program. Second, our waitlist has grown 22% and we cannot meet current demand without expanded capacity.

sections.heading: Funding Priorities for 2026
sections.body: Capacity expansion remains our top priority. We are launching a Capacity Campaign in May 2026 with a goal of $750,000 over 18 months to fund the third site, increased clinical hours, and competitive compensation for frontline staff.

sections.heading: Attendance
sections.body: Present: Daniel Marchetti (Chair), Sarah Nguyen (Secretary), Lisa Park (Treasurer), Vincent Okafor, Margaret Sutherland, Dr. James Wong, Heather Reimer (Executive Director, ex officio). Regrets: Adel Ibrahim, Karen Liu.

sections.heading: Executive Director Report
sections.body:
Heather presented Q1 program highlights. Bridges and Compass are performing strongly. Anchor is showing concerning trends in both staff retention and outcome measurement. The proposed 2026-27 budget reflects strategic reallocation toward Bridges and Compass.

Vincent asked whether the Anchor reduction was responsive to outcome data or to the program lead's repeated escalations. Heather responded that the budget decision is data-driven and predates the staffing concerns, and that the staffing concerns are being addressed separately by HR. Vincent requested that the board receive the cost-per-outcome analysis Marcus is preparing.

sections.heading: Financial Report
sections.body:
Lisa Park presented the Q4 2025 financials and the 2026-27 proposed budget. The board discussed the Anchor reduction at length. Margaret raised concern that an 18% cut may accelerate the staffing crisis Devon has flagged. Heather noted that the budget could be revisited in Q3 2026 if outcomes improve.

Lisa briefly noted the November and December petty cash discrepancies and the resolution. Brent has recommended dual-approval procedures going forward. The board approved this recommendation effective immediately.

sections.heading: Risk and Compliance
sections.body: Dr. Wong asked about Q1 incident reporting. Heather confirmed five Section 4 incidents, comparable to historical norms but trending upward in acuity. The board accepted the report.

sections.heading: Motions
sections.body:
Motion to approve the 2026-27 budget as presented. Moved Lisa Park, seconded Daniel Marchetti. Carried with one abstention (Vincent Okafor).

Motion to implement dual-approval for petty cash above $200 effective immediately. Moved Margaret Sutherland, seconded Dr. Wong. Carried unanimously.

sections.heading: Presenting Concern
sections.body:
Client M.J. is a 21-year-old presenting for an initial mental health assessment. Self-referred following GP recommendation. No prior psychiatric history.

Client reports approximately 4 months of low mood, loss of interest in previously enjoyed activities, sleep disturbance, and difficulty concentrating. Symptoms began in October 2025 following her decision to withdraw from her second year of university studies. Client describes feeling stuck and unsure of what comes next.

sections.heading: History of Present Illness
sections.body:
Client reports that the academic year began with anxiety about course content and a sense of disconnection from peers. By mid-October she had stopped attending most classes. She formally withdrew on November 14, 2025. Since withdrawing she has been living at her parents' home in southwest Edmonton. She describes feeling like a failure and like her life is on pause while everyone else moves forward.

She reports sleeping 10 to 12 hours per night but waking unrested, decreased appetite with a 4 kg weight loss over the past three months, and difficulty engaging with friends who are continuing their studies.

sections.heading: Mental Status Examination
sections.body: Client presented punctually, casually but cleanly dressed, with restricted affect and slowed speech. Eye contact was variable. Mood self-rated as 3 out of 10. Thought process was linear and goal-directed. No perceptual disturbances reported. Insight and judgment appeared intact.

sections.heading: Risk Assessment
sections.body: Client denies current suicidal ideation, intent, or plan. She acknowledges occasional passive thoughts that she would not mind not waking up but states she would not act on these and has no history of self-harm. No history of psychiatric hospitalization. Strong protective factors include a supportive family, engagement in this assessment, and absence of substance use.

sections.heading: Assessment and Plan
sections.body:
PHQ-9 score: 17, indicating moderately severe depression. GAD-7 score: 11, indicating moderate anxiety.

DSM-5-TR provisional diagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, single episode, moderate (296.22).

Weekly psychotherapy using a CBT framework, with attention to behavioural activation in early sessions. Safety plan completed and provided. Crisis line numbers reviewed. Next session scheduled February 24, 2026.

sections.heading: PHQ-9 Baseline — February 17, 2026
sections.body:
1. Little interest or pleasure in doing things — 3 (nearly every day)
2. Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless — 3 (nearly every day)
3. Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much — 2 (more than half the days)
4. Feeling tired or having little energy — 3 (nearly every day)
5. Poor appetite or overeating — 2 (more than half the days)
6. Feeling bad about yourself, that you are a failure — 3 (nearly every day)
7. Trouble concentrating — 2 (more than half the days)
8. Moving or speaking slowly enough that others have noticed — 0 (not at all)
9. Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself — 1 (several days)

Total Score: 17 — Moderately severe depression. Clinical follow-up indicated.

Functional impairment: Very difficult.

sections.heading: Subjective
sections.body:
Client reports the past week as one of the harder weeks. She describes spending most of her time in her room, sleeping during the day, and avoiding her parents. She stated "my mom keeps asking if I'm okay and I can't keep telling her I'm fine when I'm not." Client reports feeling guilty for being a burden on her family.

She notes that an old friend from high school reached out via text and she did not respond for four days. She describes feeling unable to "perform being okay" long enough for a conversation.

Client denies suicidal ideation, intent, or plan. Sleep continues to be poor in quality despite long duration. Appetite remains low.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.body: Client appeared on time, casually dressed, with minimal eye contact in the first 15 minutes of session. Affect was restricted and consistent with reported low mood. Speech was slowed but coherent. No psychomotor agitation observed.

sections.heading: Assessment
sections.body: Symptoms remain consistent with Major Depressive Disorder, moderate. PHQ-9 administered today: 16, slight improvement from intake of 17. Engagement with the therapeutic process is present despite significant depressive symptoms. No acute risk indicators present.

sections.heading: Plan
sections.body:
Introduced behavioural activation framework. Collaboratively identified one low-effort activity for the coming week: a 15-minute walk outside daily, ideally before noon. Client agreed to attempt this and to track it on a simple log provided.

Continued safety planning reviewed. Next session March 3, 2026.

case.title: Personal Journal Entries — Week of March 16-22, 2026
case.author: M.J. (patient)

sections.heading: Monday March 16
sections.body:
Walked today. 20 minutes. The sun was out which felt good for like five minutes and then I just felt tired again. Dad asked how I was at dinner and I said fine. Mom looked at me. I went to my room after.

Mood today: 4 out of 10. Walking did help a little. I don't know if it lasts.

sections.heading: Tuesday March 17
sections.body:
Didn't walk. Slept until 1pm. Felt worse. There's something about waking up that late where the day is already a failure before you even start it.

Got a text from Sarah from high school. She's at U of C now. She sent a picture of her and some friends and asked when I'm coming to visit. I haven't replied. I don't know how to tell her I dropped out.

Mood today: 2.

sections.heading: Wednesday March 18
sections.body:
Therapy today. Talked about the not-replying-to-Sarah thing. Dr. Marcoux didn't tell me what to do which is annoying and helpful at the same time. We talked about the difference between rest and avoidance. I think I've been calling avoidance rest for a while now.

Walked after therapy. 25 minutes. I cried for part of it which was weird.

Mood today: 4.

sections.heading: Thursday March 19
sections.body:
Tried to walk. Got to the door and turned around. Didn't feel like going outside. Watched videos for most of the day. Mom made my favourite pasta for dinner which I think was on purpose. I ate some. She didn't ask any hard questions. I think she's trying.

Mood today: 3.

sections.heading: Friday March 20
sections.body:
Replied to Sarah. Just said hey, sorry for the late reply, life has been weird. She replied right away and said no worries, told me about her week. I told her I withdrew from school. She said that takes guts and asked if I want to talk on the phone next week. I said maybe.

Mood today: 5. The talking helped. But I'm scared of the phone call.

sections.heading: Saturday March 21
sections.body:
Slept in again. 11 hours. Walked for 10 minutes which is nothing but it counts. Helped my mom with groceries. She didn't say anything but I could tell she liked having me around.

Mood today: 5.

sections.heading: Sunday March 22
sections.body:
Wrote in this journal looking back over the week. I noticed I had some 5s this week. I haven't had any 5s in a long time. I don't want to get my hopes up but I want to remember this when I forget.

Mood today: 6.

sections.heading: Subjective
sections.body:
Client reports a meaningful improvement over the past two weeks. She describes successfully reconnecting with a high school friend via text, including disclosing her withdrawal from university for the first time outside of her family. She notes that the response was supportive and that this surprised her.

Client identifies that the daily walk has been completed approximately 5 of 7 days for the past two weeks, an improvement from session 3 where it was 3 of 7. She notes that walking does not always improve her mood but she feels less hopeless about it.

Client reports continued sleep difficulties with consistent oversleeping. Appetite has stabilized but remains lower than baseline. She denies any suicidal ideation, intent, or plan. She notes that the passive thoughts described in the intake have become less frequent.

sections.heading: Objective
sections.body: Client presented punctually, with improved grooming and notably more spontaneous speech. Affect was reactive and showed appropriate range during session, including a smile when discussing her friend. Eye contact was sustained throughout most of the session.

sections.heading: Assessment
sections.body: PHQ-9 administered today: 12, down from 16 at session 1 and 17 at intake. This represents meaningful clinical improvement on a standardized measure. Behavioural activation appears to be having the intended effect. Therapeutic alliance appears strong.

sections.heading: Plan
sections.body: Continued behavioural activation. Added one new domain: client has agreed to attempt a brief phone call with the same friend within the next two weeks. Sleep hygiene introduced as a focus for next session. Discussed cognitive components of depression, including all-or-nothing thinking related to academic identity. Next session March 31, 2026.

sections.heading: Diagnosis (current)
sections.body: Major Depressive Disorder, single episode, moderate, improving. DSM-5-TR 296.22.

sections.heading: Progress Summary (Sessions 1-8)
sections.body:
PHQ-9 trajectory: 17 (intake), 16 (session 1), 14 (session 3), 12 (session 4), 11 (session 6), 9 (session 8).
GAD-7 trajectory: 11 (intake), 9 (session 4), 7 (session 8).

Behavioural activation has been the most consistently helpful intervention. Client now completes daily walks 6 of 7 days on average, has resumed limited contact with three high school friends, and has begun assisting her mother with errands and household tasks.

Cognitive work introduced in session 6 has been initially challenging. Client identifies a strong all-or-nothing thinking pattern around academic identity which she has begun to recognize but has not yet been able to flexibly challenge.

sections.heading: Goals — Status
sections.body:
1. Reduce depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 below 10 within 12 weeks). Status: On track. Currently 9 at week 8.
2. Re-establish at least one meaningful social connection outside the family. Status: Achieved.
3. Develop a sustainable sleep routine. Status: Partial. Sleep duration improving but quality still variable.
4. Reconnect with values and possible direction following university withdrawal. Status: In progress.

sections.heading: Risk Status
sections.body: Suicidal ideation: Currently denied. No self-harm. No identified risk factors for elevated acute risk. Safety plan remains in place and was last reviewed April 7, 2026. Will be reviewed again at session 12.

sections.heading: Plan
sections.body: Continue weekly individual sessions. Anticipated duration: 8 to 12 additional sessions, then move to biweekly maintenance phase contingent on continued progress. Client's GP has been informed of treatment with client's written consent.

sections.heading: PHQ-9 Follow-up — April 14, 2026
sections.body:
1. Little interest or pleasure in doing things — 2 (more than half the days)
2. Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless — 1 (several days)
3. Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much — 2 (more than half the days)
4. Feeling tired or having little energy — 2 (more than half the days)
5. Poor appetite or overeating — 1 (several days)
6. Feeling bad about yourself, that you are a failure — 1 (several days)
7. Trouble concentrating — 1 (several days)
8. Moving or speaking slowly — 0 (not at all)
9. Thoughts that you would be better off dead, or of hurting yourself — 0 (not at all)

Total Score: 9 — Mild depression. Continued treatment indicated. 8-point reduction from intake reflects strong clinical response.

Functional impairment: Somewhat difficult.
